We are, we're supposed to talk about, so we will talk about, putting butts in seats. And you have another session of this tomorrow night. So my part is, I thought I would talk with you some about the psychology of seminar attendance. Not so much the mechanics of marketing the seminar, although I'll show you some marketing pieces, but really the mindset of the buyer, of the prospect of the attendee, why people do, why they don't, why so few of them register, why we don't get better results than we do. What's going on that we can't affect, that we can manipulate, that drives people to either register or not register to attend an event? How many of you in here now, as part of your business, put on some kind of an event? Seminar conference of, oh everybody, okay. How many of you think like you're getting all the results you can get? Oh, okay, alright. How many of you are seeing like declining response in the last couple of years or higher cost to put the butt in the seat? Okay, a few of you. That's sort of the norm, if that makes you feel any better. It shouldn't, but sometimes the fact that others are suffering. I've had one client for the past three years, he's in a particular industry that literally has been in just free fall. And so really the only thing I've been able to do on most of the coaching calls is just tell them about somebody else's misery and make them feel better. They're really nothing to be done to the system. But so that actually is the norm. In the last couple of years, the seminar industry, if you will, if there is such a thing, has most of the people I'm in contact with have been fighting, you know, rising costs, slightly declining response. And many of them for like 10 years thought they were geniuses at this and discovered, you know, that they were benefiting from certain conditions in the marketplace and now they're challenged by certain conditions in the marketplace. But I think generally people, when they set out to market a seminar or a convention or a conference, they make the same basic mistake that we do when we set out to market products. They start and they totally focus on the thing. How many days is it? Who's speaking? What are the activities? What are we going to call it? And they don't really give enough thought to what's inside the head of the people who they want to attend. So that's where we're going to spend most of our allotted time tonight. So the first mistake, I think, is this. People underestimate the enormous resistance there is to attending. There's almost an assumption on many people's part that their herd is just dying to get on the bus and head out to the next seminar or the next conference or to their annual event. Whoever back there is running a live switch. I took the thing off the do-hickey and so we should now be seeing me. There we go. And so they start out with the assumption that people are just dying to come to another event. And for a time that may have been more true than it is now and some niches. I do a lot of work with people, for example, info marketers and the chiropractic and the dental professions. And literally the clutter there is so extreme. I know some of you in the room work those industries. I mean, if you're a chiropractor and you like seminars, there's at least 10 every weekend, maybe more that you can go to. And then there's some during the week and then there's some on evenings. And we're not even counting webinars and tele-seminars and all that. So you could spend your life in them. And actually, if you camped out like in the Anaheim Hilton and you were a chiropractor, you actually would never have to leave. You could just... Because there's enough seminars in the Anaheim Hilton that has one finished for chiropractors. There'd be another one starting and you could just go from one to the other. Every once in a while, if there was a break, there'd be some human potential metaphysical woo-woo thing that you could fill in the three days in California. So you could just live in the Hilton and go from seminar to seminar to seminar. And I mean, in many cases, in many industries, that's like the environment that we're in. In others, there's not so much clutter. But still, because you go and you are a regular goer, as well as a giver, you don't really sometimes think about how most people may not like going to the airport or they may not want to be in a hotel or they may have real problems being away from family or business so forth. So there's this enormous resistance in place when we start the process of trying to get somebody to disrupt their life and move from wherever they are to this event that we want them to be in. So I thought I would talk to you about all of the different kinds of resistance that get in the way and sort of give you a checklist to deal with because you really have to address all of them. So the first one is embarrassment and shame. So often, we are trying to get people to come to an event where they are our customer, they are our student, they are our client, and they are woefully unsuccessful and they have implemented nothing that they have gotten from us prior to this event. And this is often the case because the seminar is often where the light bulb goes on that finally gets them to do something. So this guy is sitting in his office, Rory Fatton, I used to talk about this all the time when I did a lot of marketing for Rory's events for restaurant owners. I said, so we got this restaurant owner and he is sitting in his office and our whizbang seminar brochure is in his hands. And right up above him there is a shelf. And on the shelf is a bunch of notebooks, about six blue ones, six whitens, six yellows, six maroon. Some of them got shrink wrap still on them, a bunch of them don't have shrink wrap on them, but they got a lot of stuff. The blue ones have 365 ways to market your restaurant, the yellow ones have how to create a referral culture in your restaurant, on and on and on. And guess what? He hadn't used one damn thing in any of those notebooks. Now you might think that then that is an economic issue, gee I haven't got any value out of all this stuff I already bought, but I want to go to a conference, that's not as big as he doesn't want to be the guy at the conference who hasn't done anything. And be surrounded by people, because he thinks of course he is the only guilty party, and if you do your job right that's what he should think. However erroneous that may be. So he doesn't want to be the only one sitting in the room, you know, like the only kid who comes to class who hasn't done his homework. You know, that's an unpleasant environment to put yourself in. So this embarrassment about not having implemented, not having used what he has, not having been as successful, so what do we show him when we invite him to a seminar? What's one of the things we show him? Of course, everyone else is success. We show him testimonials and success stories and case histories about everybody who's kicking butt and taking names. He's just seen pages of them, watched a DVD of them, listened to them, and now he thinks he's going to be the only guy in the room and God forbid he should get called on and do nothing. So he doesn't want to be there, right? So that's one type of resistance. Another type of embarrassment and shame resources is money. Person's got money trouble and they are not going to be able to stay in a host hotel. They're not going to be able to go to meal functions. They're going to want to buy stuff. They know people are going to try and sell them stuff. They're going to want the stuff. The people are trying to sell them and they're not going to be able to buy it, right? And so they don't want to put themselves in that environment. I can't tell you over the years the number of people who have been to an event of mine. Fortunately, the vast majority of them have then had some transformation in their circumstances, not all, but have been to an event of mine and have confessed at some point that they're all the... They get turned loose for meals. They're up in the room and brought peanut butter and non-perishable food from home because they have no money for the restaurant. They're staying down the road into Motel 6 and hiking up the driveway to the hotel. I can think of one who was sleeping in his car the whole four days at a $5,000 seminar. I can't tell you the number of times that this has happened with doctors, with not just, you know, not chueless, toothless, but I mean professional people because they're sometimes broke too. Actually, more than we would think. Most people think everybody in some other field than theirs, that everybody's making a lot of money and that's not how the pyramid sorts itself. There's that. I don't want to go there even though I really need to be there and I really want to be there, but what if I have to pick up a tab in the bar so I can't hang out in the bar and I'm not... So that's going on. Then there's feeling intimidated, feeling like they're going to be in over their head, right? So, there we go. So that's discomfort. I haven't read my book so I'm not going to know the language. Everybody's going to be ahead of me. I'm going to feel stupid even if I don't look stupid because I'm not really going to understand what's going on, right? That's another kind of resistance. Some people then immediately anticipate negative emotions. One of the first sort of specialized seminars I ever did was for my peers in speaking in 1979, I think, and a fairly prominent member of the National Speaker's Association, speaker, organized this thing, rounded up 20 people who each paid the fee to get me to come to San Francisco and do this three-day business training for them. And at the end of the three days she came up to me and said, I have never been more depressed in my entire life than I am right now. This is what you have done to me in these three days, right? And I said, gee, I mean, she's the one that organized the thing, you know? I'm saying, gee, I'm, you know. It certainly really wasn't my intent to be, I actually thought like I was motivational, you know? And she said, no, she said, I get it that this is like a real business and you got to run it like a real business and there's 512 moving parts. And I don't want to, I don't like that. And it's made me feel horrible because I only have one of the 512 moving parts and I really don't want the other 511. And so she is not the only person on the planet who has these reactions. And if they have had that reaction at event A, and now six months later we're trying to get them to come to event B, they anticipate having that reaction again. And what most people do when they know they're going to have an adverse emotional experience is they do their best to avoid it. How, let's see, how many guys in here are married? Okay? What do you do when she says we need to talk? Anything but talk, right? Anything, okay? Why? Because odds are it's not going to be good. Those conversations are usually not, you know, it's been a month since I told you everything I like about what you're doing as a husband. And I just want to sit you down and give you a praise session. Say that. Depending on how you care to count, I've been married for 22 years, 18 years, it depends whether you count the divorce or not, however you count, I never have had that conversation one single time, and I know I will die without ever having it, but I have heard we need to talk. So when someone anticipates a negative adverse emotional situation, what do they do? They get away from it. In this case, they don't register in the first place. What else? They'll be confused. So some people fear that they're going to be confused and distracted from what they're doing by all that they're shown that they're not doing. They don't want to be confused, so they don't come. Other people anticipate that they're going to get all jazzed up and then they're going to crash a few days later. So we've had that experience in the seminar room, all pumped up and excited and invigorated in pages of ideas and little post-it notes to call on their toes when they go home, and then five or six days later, they're right back the way they were. And so that wasn't a really good experience. And so that pops up into their mind now again, and they don't want that, so they don't register. Another type of resistance. Poor resistance with the host or the marketer. So I cannot tell you also, the number of times over the years, that somebody has come to me and hired me to write copy for their first conference seminar bootcamp. And I've asked questions before taking their money about what's your relationship with your customers that we're going to send this wonderful announcement of your event to. Oh, they love me, they love me. I had a great relationship. And then the mail goes and nobody registers. And then if you put somebody on the phone to start calling their customers, guess what you find out? 33% of them don't even remember who the guy is. 33% of them are thoroughly pissed off about something that was either done or not done to them six months or six years ago that has never been rectified, right? And 33% of them are even madder. Well, and then they can't figure out why they're not fill in a room. So there's a foundation to this. Before you can get somebody to put their butt in the room, there's a relationship foundation normally that has to be in place. Putting them in cold is a very, very difficult proposition. And so sometimes this is an alarm bell that then causes a change in behavior about how the customers are handled. Sunday morning at the herd building thing, I'm really talking about the care and feeding of the herd. And so one kind of resistance is, I don't have a very good relationship with this guy. And now he wants me to give him $3,000 and fly across the country and sit in a room for three days and listen to him. And I don't like him. I don't believe him. He's ignored me ever since I bought X from him. And now all of a sudden he's shown up and wants money. Poor customer service will undermine now asking them to come to a seminar, maybe more than anything else. Money is another form of resistance. They may actually have an inability to buy. Now that ability can be cured if they are sufficiently motivated because everybody can go get money from somewhere, but they may have a legitimate inability to buy. They may have difficulty securing permission to buy. So here's a big one. Almost everybody has to get permission. If they're married, they have to get permission. Now a lot of them will tell you they don't, but they do. They have to go ask. Different limits for some if it's over $10, for some if it's over $100, but just about everybody if it's over a couple thousand. I once, while married, bought a house without having a conversation about it. Or showing it to her. Had a fireplace. I knew she wanted a fireplace. That was my argument, you know. Not a good move. Not good. Not good. To the point that you have to understand if it had been her dream house, you're still going to lose this battle now over this decision. So everybody has somebody, business associate, partner, staff, mentor, or spouse, that they have to actually go get permission. Now they might never phrase it that way. They might not even think about it that way, but they are going and having to tell the story to somebody, hey, I'm taking X out of the till here, and I'm going to be gone for four days. And I'm going to be, this happens to be, by the way, personally, why I am not a big fan ever of putting an event in Vegas. There's a number of reasons, actually. One's a logistical operational thing. All of the kind of events we do and all the kind of events my clients do, we sell stuff while people are there. So it always seems to be a bad idea to be competing with Las Vegas for dollars, right? They have booze, they got hookers, we got tapes. Say, to me, to me, that's just, that's just never seemed smart, you know? But beyond that, I'm well aware that when Charlie goes to Barbara to tell the story, I need to go to this seminar, which is going to cost more than I made last month, in order to improve X, Y, and Z, and I'm going to be gone for four days, it adds to his difficulty when she says, where is the seminar, and he says, Las Vegas. What she hears when he says Las Vegas is what I said two and a half minutes ago. She hears booze and hookers, that's what just went through her head. And so we just made his job more difficult. Vice versa, we made her job more difficult if she's the one going. So it's never made a lot of sense to me. But people do have to go tell this story to somebody about the thing they are going to go to. And so now they not only have their psychological resistance, they have this other person's psychological resistance. So we have to equip them to be able to deal with that. We have the G's another seminar reaction, right? So for all sorts of reasons, theirs and that other person, well, you could skip one. Well, this is a year, money's tied, economy's not good, all the reasons that you might hear about another seminar. We have anticipated what I would call specific negative experiences, right? So one is the whole thing's just going to be a place where they try and sell me a bunch of stuff, and they're going to put a lot of pressure on me to buy stuff, so I don't want to put myself in that environment. So when you go into a store and the clerk comes up and says, may I help you, what do you say? No, why do you say no? You say, no, I'm just looking. Why? You don't want to be sold, you don't want to be put under pressure, and you say it even if you could use help. Right? You could actually use help. I did it myself just the other day. It's like a reflex action. I mean, I go to a bookstore to find some Bob the Builder stuff, never mind why. And I'm in the kids section and the clerk comes over and says, can I help? And I'm lost. I'm finding out there's Thomas the train, but I can't find the Bob the Builder. And can I help you? And reflexively I say, no, no, no, no, I'm fine. And I could have used help, right? But we react that way, same deal here. So even though people may want to go find resources, they're still A, G, I don't know if I want to put myself in that position, right? So the whole thing is about selling stuff, so that's an anticipated negative experience. Another anticipated negative experience is I'm too smart for the room, right? So I'm more advanced than most of the people that are going to be there. If I hang out with them, I'm going to be teaching for free. I'm going to be in a room with a bunch of dumb dogs. I don't want to be in a room with a bunch of dumb dogs, right? In a poor environment, if they've had that kind of experience, gee, the seating's going to be bad. I'm not going to be able to hear, my butt's going to hurt, my back, chairs, I don't sleep good, and hotel beds, okay? There's a fear of workshop thing that keeps people from registering. Fear of workshop means that people, that they're going to be forced to do workshoppy things, right? People like the, okay, now everybody turn to their, rub the shoulders of the person in front of you, and then turn to your neighbor and tell them five deep dark secrets about yourself that nobody knows. And you have seven minutes and do the exercise, right? So some people don't like that, okay? I happen to be one of them. I'm not a group participation kind of guy, you know? So some people just stay away because they fear that sort of thing's going to happen to them. There's legitimate disinterest, okay? So they're not convinced of the personal relevance of the subject matter to them. They're not convinced that they couldn't get the information easier, cheaper, faster, okay? So the speakers are going to be getting book reports. I could just stay home and read the book. They're unconvinced that the speaker is something really special. There's terminology, some I've heard it from more than one seminar goer. I'm not going to that conference because all the speakers are the usual whores. Now what the customer means by that, by the way, he meant to include me in the group, but the customer means by that is it's the same speakers on that subject matter that are at 18 conferences during the year. They're all showing up all together, they're almost in the same order on 18 different seminars. And gee, I don't want to go see them again. That's sort of legitimate disinterest, right? So that can be in the way. We're almost done. There's time, right? So legitimately, I can't be away for X days. My business is in such disarray and dysfunction that if I'm gone for three days, there's no point in going back because it will be gone by the fourth day, right? Well, a lot of business people live in exactly that environment or their personal life is in that situation. The price of the time is too high. Professionals, for example, have very high chair side value. People who make high income from doing personal work. Cosmetic dentist, cosmetic surgeon. I think about it myself. So it costs me because I could work every day writing copy or consulting at home if I wanted to. So I'm down $18,800 any day I'm not home. So it's costing me $18,800 the minute I walk out the door and go someplace else instead of staying home and working. So you multiply that times three days, that's a chunk of change. I mean, that'll take care of the vet bill at the barns for a month. So too busy to implement more, why go get more? The story about the farmer didn't want to come to the Farm Bureau meeting because he's not far and half as smart as he already knows to now. So why go get more? I haven't used what I got the last time. Somebody guilt some, right? So you already spent X amount of time. You're never home now. You're never with us now. So there's that going on. And finally now there's some resistance amongst a lot of people to travel. Rory at one time and Craig Proctor too. Is Craig in the room? You here? There you are. There you are. So Craig for those of you who don't know, like the guru to real estate agents, all 12 of them that are left in North America. So at one time, because Craig's from Canada, at one time both Rory and Craig had their boot camps in Canada and they finally moved them south into the United States. Well, they go back and forth to Canada all the time, right? They don't think it's any big deal. And by the way, it isn't any big deal. But for a lot of people, they think it's a big deal. This is a distant foreign land. I mean, they think there's moose running around up there in the streets, snow on the ground, they're going to need muckluck boots, and I don't have a passport. And so we, if you travel a lot, you don't think anything of it, but people who don't travel a lot. The last stat I saw, and I think it was a 2009 stat, it is still either, it was either 26 or 27% of the American population has never been on an airplane. They've never been on an airplane. They're still staying in there going, nah. Right? Right? Now to be fair, a bunch of them are in the Bible Belt. But I mean, seriously, they're staying in there going, nah. 28% of the people, a big percentage of people do not travel regularly. They don't do this as a normal thing. So now, I've given you a list, now think about this, a long list of all the resistance that's in place. Now not everybody has all of it, of course, hardly anybody would have all of it. But just about everybody, we're trying to convince to come to a seminar, has some of it going on at any one given moment in time inside their head. And if you look at most seminar marketing and most conference marketing, literature or websites, it doesn't address this at all. It acts as if this stuff doesn't exist. It just starts selling the event. It begins with selling the event, it ends with selling the event. It does not acknowledge that any of this stuff is in place. But it's in place, some of it, with just about everybody. So when you craft both your event itself and the marketing of the event, you want to take into account all of it, and you want to address as much of it as you possibly can. Now briefly, quickly, is an aside. Some people have the philosophy that why raise negative thoughts that may not exist? Well, A, because they exist. So pretending they don't exist is nuts. But beyond that, we're not selling in an interactive environment. If we were, if we're making a sale one-on-one, that's a different story, because now you're going to raise some of your own objections. But at a distance, we're not going to have that interactivity, so you've got to cover every base. Trying to pretend it's not there is a losing proposition. So I'll show you a few examples out of the files, some things where many of the kinds of resistance we just talked about were dealt with in one way or another. This is actually, yeah, this is a piece for Rory, for restaurant marketing systems. And first of all, what's significant is that we get to the resistance very early. So in this case, this is page one, and so I've gone right to the money resistance issue by citing very specific results tied to testimonials and telling stories. So this one's a two-week $37,000 cash flow surge. This one's $14,000 in profit. This one's 75 new customers in a week. So that's an answer to me for just about any kind of money resistance there is. I don't have it. Well, but if I knew this, then I wouldn't have that problem. So I can't afford not to go. It's also an answer to the person the story has to be told to. So now I've got to go tell Helen that I'm going to be absent from the restaurant for three days. I'm going to spend money we don't have, and I'm going to go to this thing, and Helen's going to stay back here and hold down the fort, which is another unpopular idea. But I at least got some ammo. Helen, we need a cash flow surge, $37,000 in two weeks. We need new customers, 75 new customers in a week. So I'm dealing with that resistance, and let me show you this little margin note. So this has to do with both the money and the I won't implement, I got stuff I haven't. Little margin note, I'm going to give you the ad and the strategy from Joe's two week promotion, which produced $13,500 in a week. So you're not just going to come to a seminar, you're going to go home with this thing in your hand, ready to use, that turns in to money. So I'm addressing their resistance. This is some more, well this is another money resistance thing, this is actually Tom or Renz. Tom may be here too, I think. This is 97.4% case acceptance, which in dentistry case acceptance translates to money, and this line here is pretty important, no salesmanship required. So that, now that's a kind of resistance I didn't specifically name, but so every dentist in the world would like a 97.4% case acceptance, but most dentists don't want to sell. So when you say the benefit, what's all the negative stuff that comes in somebody's mind, you better answer the negative. In this case the first thing the dentist thinks of, yeah but to get 97.4% case acceptance, I got to be a super salesman and I got to knock the patient to the ground, take the wallet out of their pocket, beat them up, scream at them, put them in a room, bring in the sales manager, etc. So we got to tell them no salesmanship required. Let's see, this is a good one. So, not another seminar. It says this is no time for any ordinary dental seminar. Not the usual whores. It doesn't say that. I'm reminding you of the kind of resistance. No, the copy is, I have personally gone underground these recent months to find and ferret out the not usual people inside our profession who are in these times achieving extraordinary mind blowing, to uncover doctors with really fresh new different even radical ideas about practice, and for the most part they keep to themselves and keep quiet about it. So that's an answer to the not the usual whores. This is another, not another seminar, same piece. The copy says, so please, well actually the subhead says, skip the usual dental seminars and save your money. So please do not schlep off to any ordinary dental seminars that aren't true the same as the previous year's dental seminars, with little more than their names or a few faces changed. Do not waste your time or money revisiting the past with a present label stuck over the top. This is what you're thinking, isn't it? Gee, think how much money can be saved by not going to seminars this year. Well, you're right, and I agree, the last darn thing you need is another, yeah, there's three paragraphs of it, because it is what they're thinking. And so we have got to emphatically convince them that this seminar is not another seminar. Regarding getting permission, big guarantee, big, bold guarantee. So again, when Helen goes to Charlie or Charlie goes to Helen and has to tell the story of going off to the seminar, right, well what if it's a big waste of time? Well, now Charlie or Helen have an answer. So this is a big guarantee, the most daring, brazen, and bold guarantee of my entire 26-year career. If by the end of day one you find me guilty of failing to uncover, reveal a handover to your real bona fide underground secrets, no, no, no, if you find me guilty you may demand whatever you deem fair, a full fee refund, or a full fee refund plus your documented airfare and lodging, or even double your fee replunded plus documented airfare and lodging. Find me guilty and you pick the punishment that you deem just, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. So this is an answer to the reader and it's ammo for the reader when he has to go and tell the story to someone else. Personal relevance, you've seen this piece, this is pretty recent, okay? So there's never been a more important time. The real important thing here is the or, okay? Probably reinvent your information marketing business or enter this highly profitable industry. Here's an answer to not the usual whores. Guidance from legitimate experts who do not just teach, okay? Let's caption up here the richest, most successful info marketers in the world and those most serious about entering the field and launching their own information businesses. Here's not another seminar by means of the total experience. You've all seen some version of this. We did it, I think, three years in a row for Rory. We had a Better Your Best contest, gave away a car, extreme restaurant and makeover. Somebody's going to qualify for that. Are you going to go home in the convertible? A lot of stuff going on. So here's the extreme lightning round, success case history, interview panel, extreme networking, only such contest in the industry. I call that three ring circus. There's a reason they don't do a one ring circus. People won't come into tent. Got to be more going on. So over here you got elephants, over here you got trapeze artists, over here you got clowns getting out of the car. The Chevy Volt. And so you got to have a lot of stuff going on. Here's, again, telling them it's not. So here's five ways this academy is profoundly different than any seminar, workshop, boot camp or other training event you've ever invested in. And then there's five reasons why it's different. Anticipated negative experience. So here's a paragraph says, read this paragraph if you're a skeptic, skip it if you're not. You might be skeptical in question whether there really is, for me, anything Kennedy you haven't heard umpteen times before, anything actually held back until now, that would be a fool's skepticism. And then there's a whole paragraph convincing them that there's stuff they haven't heard yet and I haven't shown them. By the way, this block of copy came as a result of having the first draft read by some potential attendees and having them give me some feedback. And one of the pieces of feedback was, I don't really believe that you held anything back. And I mean I've been hanging around for umpteen years and I know everything you got. So if it's in the mind of one, it's probably in the mind of others. And so now we've got to deal with that because he's anticipating a negative experience, hearing stuff he already knows, and has heard before and he thinks he's too smart for the room. Those things were all on the list I gave you. This is another denial. So this is, the subhead is, not another damn seminar. And then regarding implementation, right, because they think they're not going to go do anything, are engineering this multi-day training so that you make definite definitive decisions as we proceed and are given actual examples of every key strategy to model and depart with a complete transformation plan including short-term, medium-term, and long-term implementation in days and weeks. So we're telling them they're not going home to implement, they're going home already having implemented. The best version of that I ever saw, unfortunately it was impossible to fulfill, but it was amazing. Gary Halbert, how many of you know who Gary is? Okay, so Gary Halbert, I spoke at all of his $7,000 per person marketing seminars in Key West. And so I was invited sort of at the last minute to go on and speak one. And I was horrified when I got there and realized what the promise was to a room nearly this large. The promise was you're going to come to your three-day seminar. The seminar was nothing but hot seats, so everybody was coming up and getting a quick fix of their business with different experts on the panel depending on what your thing was. And then those experts were going in a room with you, writing a full-page ad for you, and you were leaving the seminar with the ad written, type set, formatted, and ready to place in the media. Well, it's a hell of a promise, isn't it? It's fabulous. However, by the fourth person you're so far behind, I mean it's just completely impossible, no sane person should have believed it in the first place, you know. But it was a great answer to this go-home empty-handed problem. No, your ads are going to be done, it's going to be formatted, it's fabulous, it's amazing. Let's see, I'll skip that. Experience stuff. You know, one of the big negative experiences, again, it's going to be another seminar, it's going to be an ordinary seminar, it's frankly, it's not going to be any fun, it's going to be dull, it's going to be boring. People, remember, are willing to tolerate almost anything, but not having a good time, right? So a lot of what we did for Rory was all about having a good time. This is a, so this piece is one step in a sequence for non-registerance. It's got a big nasty-looking monkey on the front of it, and the headline is, let's stop monkeying around. I'm very disappointed, you are not registered for the upcoming greatest show on Earth Boot Camp. I've sent along an inflatable monkey, and so there's an inflatable monkey in here. Okay, blows up big, good inflatable monkey, right? Since you have decided to stay home, I imagine you might want a circus monkey of your own to keep you company while you're home alone, mired in your day-to-day routine while more than 500 of the most exciting, wildly prospering restaurant owners from all over America are whooping it up in sunny Phoenix, meeting and exchanging ideas with each other, winning prizes, meeting George Foreman, going inside the life in times of promotional genius, PT Barnum, getting solid how-to, make more money, information, or greatest lineup of experts I've assembled in six years. While we're sharing all that experience, you can be home with your inflatable monkey. Now, it's... And then there's a page that looks like this. Are you still sure you want to stay home with your monkey? That was not... See, it sort of makes a tonality statement, doesn't it? You know, besides laying it on him about staying home, but it kind of makes a tonality statement. These are testimonials that... So, you've seen a piece like this before where there's a list of everybody who's already registered, right? But then each one of the testimonials that we chose deals with a different type of resistance that was on my resistance list. So, this one, I won't make this mistake again. I purchased the program in 2001 before there was a boot camp to go to and had a hard time getting started. I was confused. I was having trouble implementing the strategies, and so I made the wrong decision and decided not to go to the first-ever boot camp. And I thought about it often from June of 2002 to January when I had a chance to go. Then next time, I still made the wrong decision and passed on it. All the reasons are excuses. I guess I thought I knew enough, and then finally we get to the punchline of finally went and discovered how little they knew and revolutionized. This one is... Well, this is just an answer to Small Town. Let's see. This is too smart for the room, so this is pity the restaurateur with all the answers. I was one of them, and this guy goes on to tell about how successful he already was, but he was working too hard and didn't know it could be easy. I was leery about coming and spending that kind of money because we've been in business for 21 years and we're very successful. So every one of the testimonials in this piece was chosen to address a different kind of resistance on the list and the headline above each testimonial let you find the one that was matched up with your particular kind of resistance. Three Ring Circus. This is... you get a sense of it. So what's happening at the... this was the magic one. So this was... we dressed Rory up like a magician. I think Dave Dees here, and I think Dave was at this one. So again, there's all sorts of things going on. There's celebrities, there's a car giveaway, there's... we pulled a rabbit out of a hat panel, there's a top secret Mrs. X, there's Rory doing something, there's magical networking, there's an optional restaurant tour the night before the boot camp everybody gets on buses and goes around to fascinating restaurants, all kind of moving parts. All sorts of stuff going on so that clearly this is not an ordinary sit in a room and be lectured to seminar. You guys probably all remember, I think maybe the best super conference piece of all the ones we've done was the one for the year we had Gene Simmons. And again, so this picture's proof, right? There's a ton of people there, therefore there'll be a ton of people there, therefore you should be there. Again, a lot of moving parts, there was a car, may I give you the keys, there was a car giveaway that year, there was of course Simmons, there was seminar within a seminar. This is... oh, you got to make your speakers relevant. So remember, one of the kinds of resistance is personal relevance, right? So you want a celebrity speaker, because people like to have their picture taken with a celebrity, they like to see a celebrity, they like to hear from a celebrity. But you got to go out of your way to make the celebrity relevant, otherwise you're leaving like half the value behind. So when I write copy, I'm always about how are we going to take this guy? And so in many cases, so like at Rory's, we had Joe Vitale speak, many of you probably know Joe, and Joe has a book called, There's a Customer Born Every Minute, which is about how P.T. Barnum would market your business. So I made a piece, of course, how would P.T. Barnum promote your restaurant? Because these guys are restaurant owners, and if you don't say restaurant, they don't get it. When we had Famous Amos Speak, we took all the Famous Amos books, took stuff out of it, rewrote it, and made a little book that went out with the promotional package. This is another, I think this is just another, oh this is kind of interesting. It's a miracle we client didn't get sued, but so this was for the circus year, so this is a Dr. Seuss kind of brochure. I wrote poetry. So that's why I get the big bucks. So this whole thing is done in Dr. Seuss style, presenting everything that's going on at the seminar. I think this is one where we shot Rory out of a cannon at the beginning of the seminar too. No, I'm deadly serious. One of them we shot him out of a cannon, the other one we had him right in and an elephant. This is, so here's Famous Amos, so we took Famous Amos' book cover, and then we rewrote the book inside, and we added restaurant testimonials and made 12 principles of how you can build your restaurant, so making the speaker relevant because that's an issue. So if you go back to the list of all of the kinds of resistance, and you may have extra ones that apply to your folks that don't necessarily apply to everybody, the correct approach to this is to figure out every kind of resistance in the way of their registration, and then address it in as many different ways as you can, and pay at least as much attention to, give at least as much emphasis to, that to what's going on in their head as they try not to buy. You understand even the ones, except the very easy ones. So almost everybody who's marketing to a herd to have a relationship again and again and again, there's a relatively small percentage who know they're coming. You could send them a one-page fax and say, here's when the event is, sign here and you'd get them. But setting them aside, the most important thing I can tell you in summation of all this is even the ones who are your pretty good customers are starting out trying not to register. That's where they're at, because there's a lot of reasons not to. So they're starting trying not to register. You need to start where they start. If you're starting with, here's the thing, you're way ahead of them. They're still back here with all this stuff. What they buy, it's a writer downer. What they buy isn't why they buy. So when they buy a seminar, and it almost doesn't, it doesn't matter how serious it is, it doesn't matter if it's 90 minutes or if it's three days, none of that matters. When they buy a seminar, they're buying a seminar. So what is a seminar? Well, it's basically a learning experience, right? It's adult education. If they buy a conference, what is a conference or a convention? Well, it's like a business gathering where people in the same industry or the same field come together and exchange information and ideas and maybe go to the exhibit hall. So that's what they're buying. It is not why they're buying at all, and we want to deal with why they're buying. Different people are nudged over by different things, but there's basically six or seven different categories of motivations for buying and coming to an event. So the first is really the fun stuff, right? And they might not enunciate it or admit it, but it's really important to most people that they're going to have a great entertainment experience, right? They're coming for the cocktail party story. They're going to take home. They're coming for the photo opportunities. They're coming to see whoever your Shamu the whale is, right? And just as it aside, Shamu the whale money is almost always money well spent. Now it helps. It's beneficial. And in our case, we work very hard at it managing to find a Shamu, a celebrity, who is also relevant, who also has something useful to say. That's a tough criteria. It's not always possible, but it's also not always necessary. Sometimes relevance doesn't matter, but if it's somebody cool that people want to see and people want to get a chance to meet and people want to get their picture taken with, that can be enough. You can stretch relevance to just barely there. When we did the, borrowed from Yannick, when we did the underground thing for Tamarant and Dentistry, oh no, the year we did the spy thing, excuse me, we used Robert Fawn from the Man from Uncle. And he had nothing relevant to dental practices to say. It didn't matter, right? The theme of the whole thing was spy. He was a spy. People 45 plus, Man from Uncle. It's cool to see him. That was enough. But Shamu the whale money spent is generally money well spent. So that category is the fun stuff. Then there's the category of getting together. So a lot of event attendance is really driven by fraternity, right? It's a place to get back together with a bunch of people you know, but who are scattered all across the country, who you enjoy seeing, being with, exchanging ideas with, hanging out with. And this is a convenient time and a place to do it at your event. Now the key to having that happen is to make sure you make it happen in the preceding event. So this builds on itself. Fraternity forms again and then strengthens and strengthens and strengthens. So you got to make sure you're creating enough opportunities for people to mix and meet and form fraternity if you want fraternity to be a driving force for the next event. Ideas and information sharing all falls into that category. Next category then are activities. What they do while they're there. And what's most important to understand about that is, even though perhaps we should want to come, sit in a chair, never move from the chair for as many hours as we can stay awake and collect as much information as we possibly can that does not motivate most people. I will tell you there was a time you could sell that. Decade or so ago, I used to sell that pretty aggressively. No frills, no entertainment, no fluff, no food, no nothing. Sleep before you get there. Don't plan on sleeping while you're there. We're going to start a dawn. We're going to go to midnight. We got a lot to... I used to sell that. It's not a good thing to sell now. It's 10 years later, the public is just not there. They just are not there. So activities, a range of activities. So people like to know that there's going to be different things going on, that they're going to have options. That's why breakout session choices are good. Roundtables, where they get to go around and talk to different experts are good. That they're going to have some freedom outside the classroom sometime. And some people are motivated purely by the escape. In the chiropractic profession years ago, we used to have a saying about the chiropractic seminars that we gave. We used to jokingly inside call them alibis are us. Because for a certain number of people, they were buying an alibi. All the way down to some of them who checked in and were never seen again until closing ceremonies. Okay, by me, that $5,000 spends just as good as anybody else's $5,000. If you want an alibi, I'll sign right here. You bet, he was in the seminar the whole time. Right, right there. Just run his card again, because he's not going to question the double chart. No, I'm kidding. But some people are really motivated by an excuse, a reason, an acceptable reason to somebody else to get out of dodge and know that they're going to go get out of their daily routine, get out of their business. So that's important. The fourth category has to do with place. And again, the conversation of location is a long, complex conversation. I know Bill's going to address some of it tomorrow night. I mean, personally, again, I wouldn't put one in Vegas. There are certain industries where definitely the place to put it is Vegas, because it dramatically increases attendance. If you have a lot of families, if you have a lot of mom and pops in your niche, Orlando is a great place to put it, because they can now tag Disney on before or after. If you're dealing with, if you're putting on the National Convention of Strip Club Owners, Orlando probably not the place. Most of them are coming with all with their wife and kids. So Vegas is probably the place. Place matters. I happen to be a believer in isolation. Not everybody is. So quite candidly, we're at me, you wouldn't be here, because you're too close to a bunch of restaurants and bars and stuff that you can walk to. Not what I usually do. I want you stranded on my island, and it's very inconvenient for you to go anywhere else. So it tells. Ideally, there's only one central bar, not ten bars, because you want everybody running into each other. But from the marketing standpoint, from the standpoint of telling the story, place matters, and not only the physical location, but what the place is going to be like matters. Exhibitors. A reason why a lot of people go to conferences, conventions, seminars is to find vendors. The Exhibitor is a legitimate activity. It's a legitimate value added, and you ought not necessarily eliminate it. Resources and tools, gifts and prizes. People like to get stuff. They like to go home with stuff. They really like to get stuff free. So that can be a big plus. Next big category is a place to be important. Even successful business owners, successful professionals, generally speaking, are not getting a lot of applause at home. No matter how well they're paying everybody and how well their business is doing, when they come in on Monday morning, generally speaking, the staff does not line up, applaud and give them a plaque. It doesn't happen very often. I've had as many as 48 employees, had one for a long time. I actually do occasionally. I don't get any applause from Vicki, but every once in a while, she sends me a dirty joke. Well, I mean, it's something, you know? But I can't recall any of them ever lining up applauding. It doesn't happen. And so most people are not getting it at home. They're not getting it in their business. They are not getting recognition. They are not necessarily being treated as important. And so a place where they get to come and be an important VIP. And they get to be in where the better ribbon, and they get to be in the VIP lounge, or they get to come up on stage and get an award, or they get recognized from the front of the room. They get pointed out as a seasoned veteran, and then a bunch of other people go ask them questions, and that makes them feel smart. That's a reason why people come. Now, you can't say it exactly the way I just said it when you're selling it, but you better make sure they're aware of it as part of the total experience. I wanted to talk just for a minute about the issue of the institutionalized event. So there's pros and cons to this. Many of you have them because perhaps you've copied us. The association market, of course, has them. So this is the annual convention, or the annual conference that reoccurs, and everybody knows it reoccurs, and it happens pretty much at the same time every year. So it's an institutionalized event. And Glaser Kennedy, of course, the super conference is that, and the Info Summit is that. They occur every year. They occur pretty much at the same time every year. And although they get changed up a lot, fundamentally, the format and the purpose is the same. When this works, it works marvelously well. So in chiropractic, for many years, when I was doing a lot of work in chiropractic, I spoke for three years, I think, in a row. It still exists. There's a thing called Parker. We have chirops in the room. Do you have any chiropractors? How many of you are still, like, going to Parker? Like, it's the thing to go to. Not as many. There was a time when if you were a chiropractor, you went to Parker every year. That's what chiropractors did. And chiropractors would be asking each other three months before, are you going to Parker? You're going to Parker, aren't you? Going to Parker? Going to Parker. I'll see you at Parker. I mean, it's what you did. In speaking, the National Speakers Association, if you're an NSM, you go to the convention. That's what you do. And you're weird if you're not going. So Parker would have thousands of chiropractors there, and they all went every single year, and they really didn't have to do a hell of a lot of marketing. I mean, they put out a brochure, and here's who's going to be here, and everybody went, because they're supposed to go. It's what you do if you are one of them. When that works, it's like gold, gold, because there's a predetermined expectation at the time the event is announced that you're gone, right? When it doesn't work, it's really bad. So there's pros and cons to trying to make it work that you should be aware of. The pros are, of course, that people know they are supposed to attend. There's peer pressure to attend. I'll see you there. You're going, but what do you mean you're not going? How could you not be going? Everybody's going. So there's peer pressure to attend. There's reoccurring fraternity. So everybody knows everybody's going to be there. And so the buddies I got that are not really buddies, because we live at opposite ends of the country, we're going to see each other there. So most are pre-interested, and leaning toward registering has the first reaction to the promotion. So instead of starting with all this resistance, they're actually starting nearly predetermined to register because it's the thing you're supposed to do. It's the thing everybody does, and peers are going to think less of me if I'm not there. It also lends itself to a long sales cycle, which allows you, if you wish, to do monthly installments and make the monthly payment pretty low, because you can stretch it over six or seven months. There are a few information marketers who have one institutionalized, annualized event who literally have people on permanent continuity for the event. So they just get charged every month, and they're coming to the event every year, and that's that, unless somebody squeals stop. The negatives to this approach are, first of all, it's easy for it to drift into same old, same old. It's easy for the person putting it on, the company putting it on, to get a little lazy about making it fresh and making it exciting every year, because there's this expectation that everybody's supposed to come and everybody's going to come. You're vulnerable to the new entry into the market, who has got a fresh face and fresh ideas and is very seductive because they've got something new. You can find yourself under significant up the ante pressure year from year to year. So if you've got Shamu this year, who's your bigger Shamu next year, and then you've got to have three Shamus, and pretty soon, you know, you've got to have a whole damn SeaWorld. And so there's the up the ante pressure. There's the enormous temptation to say, I'll just skip this year, okay? Because I know it occurs every single year. And it can be tough to get early registrations because everybody tends to wait until the last minute. It's hard to convince them that there's a shortage because they know you do it every year and everybody shows up. So those are the pros and cons of the institutionalized event. Sort of the last thing I have for you before we run out of time is to talk a little bit about who attends. Don't squint, I'll tell you. This is as much me as you, but the big type you can make the list from. So who attends? Well, one kind of person who attends is often the brand new guy, the newbie. The customer, it's their first event. Obviously for everybody, it's a first event at some point. They got to come to their first one before they can come to the second one. The big obstacle is if it's close to their first purchase, they think they're not ready. A strategy is some sort of special activities just for the newbies. So for several years in a row with the Info Summit, you may recall we had the day before the A to Z Blueprints Day, which now has been replaced with online training, but so if you were new, you came to that extra day and you got your orientation so you were up to speed with everything by the time the event started. It can be that, it can be a first-timers reception, but if you're going to have a lot of newbies, if you have a lot of first-timers, if you want a lot of first-timers, the strategy is something special for them. The long-time customer, but first-time attendee. So if you have a lot of people in your customer base, if you're an established Info Marketer, you've been at this for a while and you've done umpteen events and you have a bunch of customers who are good customers, they bought from you, they buy from you again, but you can never get them to an event, you got to address reasons why this person who appears to be happy buys product again and again, but never comes to an event why they haven't come. The gimmick is again some sort of special activities and or special recognition just for them. So advanced sessions that only customers who've been with you for five years or longer can go to, some special recognition, longevity awards, etc. The lost inactive customer ought not be ignored. Many, many Info Marketers are able to get people who've dropped out of continuity, they haven't bought a product in three years, four years, five years, they're like lost customers are able to reactivate them with a great event. Two things you need to know, one, you got to give them amnesty, meaning they can't feel any kind of embarrassment or shame or anything about returning to the fold after having left. Secondly, even more important, a good gimmick is to give them for free what they don't want to pay for as the bonus with attending the event. So for example, if they've dropped out of continuity and they sort of refuse to stay in continuity, go ahead and give them a year or two of whatever you're delivering in continuity as the bonus when they register for the event. They don't want to pay for it, but they'll take it for free. Practitioners of the slight edge, they are the ones who come to everything, they are the ones who by first generally, they're usually the most successful people you have in your herd, there's a closed loop of that behavior, they are really slight edge people, they are saying to themselves, if I just get one thing, it's okay, if I'm there for three days and I just get one thing that's really useful that I can go use, if I tighten a screw here or there and my thing, that works out to a lot of money, I'm happy. So the best thing to do with them is go to them first before you go to anybody else and give them recognition for who they are. Serious students, they're a small percentage of almost every group, they also tend to be the most successful, they really are interested in content. So to them you've got to make a content story. There is the ungenerous way to describe them as seminar junkies, meaning they're the guy that checks into the Hilton and just stays there and goes from one seminar to the next, or the more pleasant way is, they're at least at yours every year. A, you've got to make it a point of pride to do that. You want to encourage that behavior with recognition. And B, you still have to figure out a way to make each event, each reoccurring event, feel like it's new and fresh and different. I worked for a number of years where we did a three and a half day event every weekend and had to get 800, 900, 1,000 people, bought two thirds of them the same every weekend. So every Monday we're thinking of how to describe Friday as something entirely new and different and groundbreaking than the one everybody just walked out of. Everyone was a groundbreaking, never to be repeated, never seen again event. It's good pressure. I mean, you learn from the pressure. Another type of people to come, social animals. So they really are there. You may not even be able to keep them in the room. They're really there for the social, the fraternity, the activity. Be sure you give it to them and let them know ahead of time that they're going to have those opportunities. There are the customers who are coming who are trying to figure it out. They know they don't get it. They're hoping the light bulb is going to go off. They're not able to implement. They know they haven't been able to implement. Our strategies for them are again to acknowledge that they exist and to promise them solutions to that particular problem, that they're going to get blueprints. They're going to get roadmaps. They're going to meet vendors who can do it for them. They're going to have Q&A opportunity. They're going to have hot seats. Somehow they're going to get one-on-one mentoring. They're going to get to go sit at tables with experts and ask their questions. They're coming to try and figure it out. You've got to make sure they know they're going to have an opportunity to figure it out. The last ones that come are the ones who are going down for the third time and reaching up for a life preserver. They're in real trouble. They're in financial trouble, probably. Their businesses are not doing well. They're looking for the magic rescue. A, if you have one, be sure you promise it. B, you want to tell turnaround and rescue stories in your marketing because they're there. Don't ever think, by the way, that the person who can't afford it won't buy it and come. I'm going to look over here so I don't make eye contact with anybody because I don't know. But I promise you in this room, there are at least a few who absolutely cannot afford to be here. But they're here anyway. Now, hopefully they do the right things as a result of what we've been here and they never have to suffer that. Again, I have no control over that. Only they do, but I promise you they're in the room. I promise you they had to run it on eight credit cards. They had to go borrow it from their mother-in-law. They had to sell something to be able to be here. Don't ever think they're not in your herd and don't ever think they won't come. And so they need to be addressed too. And so if you have great turnaround testimonials, if you have great rescue testimonials, if you have even one dramatic human interest story of the person who was one step away from eviction and suicide and they scraped together bottle caps in order to get to your thing, and they tell the story. And tell the story every time, by the way, even if you're sick of it, because they're out there and they need to hear it. So finally, think of the instant reactions that happen when you arrive to offer somebody a seminar. There's some small percentage who say they're waiting. Oh boy, great, where do I sign up? Then there's some who want to go, but they've got to justify it to themselves. They've got to justify it to somebody else. There's some who are cautiously interested. They're like, if you've ever tried to feed a squirrel in your backyard, you know, you can't make any fast move. That's where they are. There's some who are instantly, probably not. I'm not going to go. I'm not going to go this year. But I'll listen to the pitch. I'll come on the tele-seminar. Even though I'm not going, I'll come on the tele-seminar. So there's some of them. There's some who are unknown, another seminar, and then there's some who instantly, no, I can't, I shouldn't, I won't. He or she won't let me. I promised them I won't go to another one. So there's this wide range of instant reactions when you arrive and say, come to a seminar, and you have to address all of them because we don't have any interactivity. Hey, do we have anybody who can run a mic? Do we have any ability to run a microphone around? Do you want somebody to ask questions? Somebody? We'll just pick a section of the room so you don't run all over the place. We'll let, do we have somebody? Because I got like seven minutes left, so we might as well use it. I was good at trying to do Q&A. Oh, we got a mic right there. Well, what are we talking about? Can we turn that puppy on? Does that work? There's one there too. Look at that. We're all set up for questions. Oh, not you, for Christ's sake. Do you understand? Huh? Are those things functional? If you can figure out how to make it work, you can ask your question. Okay. Go, go, go, go. Time's wasting. All right, so we are not our customers. Well, maybe you are, maybe you aren't. I don't know. Okay, and you've been talking about achieving, getting into their minds, and then being able to write and structure stuff to answer what they're thinking about. So, if we are not our customers, and if we don't think the way they do, how do we achieve that mindset? Well, you either know them very well, or you now got to go gather that. That's why selling cold, selling seminars cold is very difficult. I mean, the best thing is when you're dealing with a market. So, he sent up a gift to me, so he must be here. Is TJ a roll letter here? See here somewhere? TJ? No? He sent a gift up to me upstairs, so he must be here somewhere. So, TJ, if you don't know TJ, do you know? Yeah. Okay. So, TJ writes for the blue collar opportunity seeker market. So, you know Al Bundy on Marry With Children? Okay. So, Al Bundy, but 15 years older, is roll letters customer. Right? TJ hits it better than anybody I've ever seen, because he was an opportunity seeker answering all the ads, buying all the courses, buying all the systems, trying one business after another, and he's never lost his connection with them. He like has them, and so he writes like he's hiding under their kitchen table and hearing their conversation, you know, at midnight. Halbert, you could not beat a Halbert control. I don't know anybody that ever did, when he was writing to quote quote every man. Ron LeGrain. It's right here? Right around? So, Ron brought into platinum one time, five ads, laid him on the table, said, which one do you think, you know, worked better, and now all the platinum members are looking at him, and I picked the right one, but not actually because I thought it was, if I hadn't known it was a Halbert ad, I would have picked one of the other ones because it didn't seem like the best ad, but I recognized his Halbert style. Nobody's gonna beat him. Okay, he connected with that person. So the best thing is if you were one of them, or you really live among the tribe and are sensitive enough to really know where they are, failing that, you've got to kind of do focus groups, you've got to have conversations, you've got to go hang out where they are, you've got to pay attention to them, but you really, this is about where they are, not where you or I are, what we want to sell them. Okay, cool. Okay, don't anybody else walk up, we're gonna do you and that's gonna be it. Okay, now this time you got in here legal, right? Yeah. Yeah. Well, this is my idea of entrepreneurial initiative. So we were having the Cleveland chapter meeting and Dan Cricks has an annual thing at the racetrack where I raced my horses and so he drove from Buffalo to come to the meeting and then somehow he conned his way past security into the back stretch area into the paddock where you are not supposed to be without a license. He actually got security to bring him there. And so I'm walking in the paddock and there he is, which he is not supposed to be. So I consider that admirable personally. So I say I think that's it. Thank you. But here I see you have a badge so unless it says Susan on it, we assume that you got in here. Go ahead. Thank you. As you're addressing each of these points of resistance, how do you decide when to address it in the sales process? Well, it's a very good question for which there is no good answer. First of all, you got to try and rank them in the way that you think they will be most important to most of your prospects. So which is the biggest piece of resistance? Which is the second biggest piece of resistance? And then you've got to find places to naturally get to them. So it's not so much we're going to do them all on page three. It's not so much we're going to do them after we cover this. I don't want you anymore if you're looking up here now. I was looking for you to a grand five minutes ago. I don't need you now. So it's not that simple. It's where do they fit in the story that we're telling? And that might be right at the beginning. It might be right at the end. It might be somewhere else. And it might be more than once addressed in different ways. So like we can address them through testimonials. We can address them through a story. We can address them directly with sales copy. We can do multimedia. So some of them might be dealt with in print. Some of them might be dealt with in audio. But you generally don't want to hold up a sign and say, here's the 12 kinds of resistance. But sometimes you come close to that. The four reasons why people won't attend. So it's not as simple as where do they go. Okay? Yes, sir. First of all, thank you for the seminar. Welcome to Maryland. Okay. The question is, how does this dynamic change if the event is free? I host an event a couple of times a year, between 100 to 200 people register, but the event itself is free. There is no admission fee. I got an echo. I'm having trouble. Okay. Oh, if it's free. What a great question. Okay? No one should ever think it's easier to sell free than it is to sell paid. And so many of us use free seminars in one way or another, a preview, a free tele-seminar, a free webinar, and often the assumption is, well, because it's free, we don't know. For whatever combination of reasons, putting their butt in the seat for free, for 500, for 5,000, all the same dynamics exist. The money resistance isn't there, but all the other resistance is there. So the dynamics really don't change at all. Free does not necessarily make it easier. No, you don't get a follow-up, because now I'm overtime. So you and then we're done. Yeah, I'll be quick. In my industry, the prospects have a lot of faith in the association. So there's an association with just 4,000 members. They do a direct mail campaign. They fill up a room with 700 people. My question is, as an information marketer, other than starting my own association, perhaps, how can I come close to getting that level of trust? Well, I'm going to tell you several things real quick. One is, you might consider looking for some sort of relationship with the association. Sometimes that can be done. Sometimes it can't be done. Associations these days are very much in need of what are called non-dues revenue. And so money does talk. And if, in fact, you're correct, and they are beloved and highly respected and all that, they may be worth renting in one way, shape, or form. The other thing I'll tell you is, is that in most cases, what is true about an association membership as a market is there's some percentage who are actually completely dissatisfied and frustrated with what they are getting from the association. And a very opposite positioning will resonate with them. Now, it will repel others, but it will really resonate with them. Only you can figure that out in your situation. But sometimes that's the play. Hey, folks, hope we had a good night. Give me as much as I can. Thank you very much.
